posts.title: The Power of Doing Nothing
posts.excerpt: The hardest thing in markets is not buying or selling. It's doing nothing. There's a famous observation in investing: some of the best-performing accounts were the ones forgotten or untouched for years.

posts.title: Common Investing Biases: Not Cutting Losses
posts.excerpt: I held a losing position longer than I should have, hoping time would fix it. It wasn't about capital — it was about ego. Exiting hurt less than staying wrong. Losses are the fee we pay to learn. Cut them early, before they cut your capital.

posts.title: On Market Sentiment
posts.excerpt: Markets are often driven by sentiment, not fundamentals in short term. When an asset rises sharply in a short time, it's usually held by weak conviction. In the first sign of stress, profits get booked first where gains are the most and easiest. Prices that run on emotion tend to fall hardest in a crisis.

posts.title: Common Investing Biases: FOMO (Fear of Missing Out)
posts.excerpt: Usually bad buys start with, "What if I miss this?" FOMO replaces analysis with urgency. You chase price, not value. If an idea only works when you rush, it probably doesn't work.

posts.title: On Trusting Your Decisions
posts.excerpt: The hardest part of investing isn't analysis. It's trusting your own judgment. I've seen how easy it is to trust someone else's decision, and how painful it feels to regret not trusting your own. Self-made decisions carry responsibility and learning. Ownership accelerates growth.

posts.title: On Portfolio Management
posts.excerpt: The biggest investing mistake is copying someone else's risk. I can take calculated risks my father cannot — not because one of us is smarter, but because our lives demand different outcomes. As responsibilities grow, protecting capital matters more than chasing returns.

posts.title: On Choosing Mutual Funds
posts.excerpt: Most investing mistakes come from using the wrong tool for the goal. Long-term goals (5–10 years) suit equity mutual funds. Short-term needs — a vacation, a phone, planned expenses — need safety, not volatility like a debt fund.

posts.title: Common Investing Biases: Holding Without Hope
posts.excerpt: I've held a losing stock even after I stopped believing in the business. Waiting for a specific price to exit is emotion disguised as discipline. If there's no fundamental upside, time doesn't repair the decision. Capital belongs in better opportunities. The goal isn't to recover losses from one stock — it's to make money from the market.

posts.title: On Time in the Market
posts.excerpt: Years can deliver negative returns. Decades never have. Short periods test patience. Long periods reward fundamentals. If the business is sound and the time frame is long, the real work is staying invested. Time compounds. Discipline lets it work.

posts.title: On Support and Resistance
posts.excerpt: I use support and resistance to define a range for buying and selling. These levels matter because large investors often have their average buying or selling around these zones. When prices move near them, capital tends to respond — either defending the level or reassessing it. The goal isn't precision.

posts.title: On Using Moving Averages
posts.excerpt: I use daily moving averages over long periods — a year or more — not to predict, but to observe trends. Levels like the 100-day moving average often act as reference points: how a stock behaves around them tells me more than the level itself. Does it respect the trend and recover? Or does it fail repeatedly? The insight isn't in the indicator. It's in the behavior over time.

posts.title: On Market Regrets
posts.excerpt: The market will make you regret almost every decision. Sell at a profit — the stock goes higher. Buy the dip — it dips further. Regret isn't a signal. It's noise. The mistake is judging decisions by outcomes instead of process. You'll never catch the top or the bottom. Be satisfied with disciplined decisions, not perfect ones.

posts.title: On Fundamentals and Timing
posts.excerpt: Fundamentals tell me what to buy. Technical charts tell me when to buy. One answers quality. The other manages timing.

posts.title: A Bias That Cost Me Money
posts.excerpt: I continued buying at lower prices in a losing stock longer than discipline allowed — not because the business and cash flow statements improved, but because I wanted that stock to recover my loss. The objective is not to make money from a particular stock. The objective is to make money from the market. Capital doesn't recover emotions. It compounds where fundamentals allow.

posts.title: On Markets
posts.excerpt: In the short term, prices move on charts and sentiment. In the long term, they follow the company's profits and cash flows. Noise fades. Business economics always remain.

posts.title: Why I'm Learning Asset Management
posts.excerpt: I didn't come to finance for stock tips or market predictions. I came for decision-making. Over the last few years, I've invested my own money and helped a few people start their investing journeys. Some decisions worked, some didn't. What stayed with me was not the returns, but how process, risk, and behavior mattered far more than confidence or effort. I'm interested in asset management because it rewards clear thinking over noise, patience over activity, and humility over certainty. This newsletter is a public journal of how I learn: how I analyze businesses, how I think about risk, where I get things wrong — and why. No recommendations. No promises. Just documented thinking over time.
